meta.name: Build Your Own SQLite
meta.description: Embedded SQL database with tokenizer, parser, bytecode compiler (VDBE), page-based B-tree/B+tree storage, buffer pool, query planner, and ACID transactions via rollback journal and WAL.
meta.essence: SQL tokenization and recursive-descent parsing producing ASTs, compiled into bytecode instructions executed by a virtual machine (VDBE), operating over a page-based storage engine with B-trees for clustered table storage and B+trees for secondary indexes, managed through a buffer pool with LRU eviction, and ACID guarantees via either rollback journal or write-ahead logging.
meta.why_important: Building a database from scratch teaches the fundamental data structures and algorithms underlying all modern databases. These skills—disk I/O management, B-tree indexing, bytecode execution, query optimization, and crash recovery— are directly applicable to backend engineering, distributed systems, and performance optimization roles.
learning_outcomes: Build a lexer and recursive-descent parser converting SQL text into an AST
Compile ASTs into bytecode instructions for a virtual machine (VDBE)
Implement a buffer pool manager with LRU eviction and dirty page tracking
Design page-based B-tree storage for tables (clustered) and B+tree for indexes
Implement row storage with variable-length record encoding
Build a cost-based query planner with statistics-driven cardinality estimation
Implement ACID transactions with rollback journal for crash recovery
Design WAL mode for improved concurrent read/write performance

milestones.name: SQL Tokenizer
milestones.description: Build a lexer that converts SQL text into a stream of typed tokens.
acceptance_criteria: Tokenizer recognizes SQL keywords (SELECT, INSERT, CREATE, WHERE, JOIN, etc.) case-insensitively
String literals enclosed in single quotes are parsed including escaped quotes ('it''s' → it's)
Numeric literals including integers and floating-point values (42, 3.14, -7) are recognized as distinct token types
Operators (=, <, >, <=, >=, !=, <>) and punctuation (comma, parentheses, semicolon) are tokenized as distinct tokens
Identifiers (table names, column names) support quoted identifiers with double quotes ("column name")
Tokenizer reports error position (line and column) for unrecognized characters
Token stream correctly tokenizes at least 20 diverse SQL statements in a test suite
Tokenizer recognizes keywords like SELECT and INSERT case-insensitively
String literals correctly handle escaped single quotes ('') and preserve internal content
Numeric literals distinguish between integers (42) and floats (3.14)
Double-quoted identifiers ('"Table Name"') are correctly tokenized as identifiers
The tokenizer returns a list or stream of objects containing type, value, line, and column
A test suite of 20+ SQL statements produces the expected token sequence
pitfalls: Not handling escaped quotes in string literals ('it''s') causes premature string termination
Keywords must be case-insensitive but identifiers may be case-sensitive depending on quoting—handle both
Unicode identifiers require careful handling; start simple with ASCII and document limitations
Negative numbers may be ambiguous with subtraction operator; handle at parser level, not tokenizer
concepts: Lexical analysis converts character stream to token stream
Finite state machine drives character-by-character token recognition
Token types classify each lexeme (keyword, identifier, literal, operator)
Error reporting with source location enables useful diagnostics
deliverables: Lexer producing token stream from SQL input string
Keyword recognition for all supported SQL words
String and numeric literal parsing with escape handling
Operator and punctuation tokenization
Error reporting with line and column position
Test suite with at least 20 SQL statements

milestones.name: SQL Parser (AST)
milestones.description: Build a recursive-descent parser that converts the token stream into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
acceptance_criteria: SELECT parser produces AST with column list (including *), FROM clause, optional WHERE, ORDER BY, and LIMIT
INSERT parser produces AST with target table, optional column names, and VALUES clause
CREATE TABLE parser extracts column definitions with names, types (INTEGER, TEXT, REAL, BLOB), and constraints (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
Expression parser correctly handles operator precedence: NOT > comparison (=,<,>) > AND > OR
Parenthesized expressions override default precedence
Parser produces clear error messages with token position for syntax errors
Test suite covers at least 15 valid and 10 invalid SQL statements
SELECT parser produces AST with column list, FROM, and optional WHERE/LIMIT
INSERT parser handles target table and VALUES mapping
CREATE TABLE parser extracts column names, types, and constraints (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL)
Expression parser correctly handles NOT > AND > OR precedence
Parenthesized expressions correctly override default precedence levels
Parser provides error position (line/column) for syntax errors
Test suite passes for 15+ valid and 10+ invalid SQL edge cases
Tokenizer output is correctly consumed as parser input (token stream integration)
SELECT parser produces AST with column list including * wildcard support
SELECT parser produces AST with FROM clause containing table name
SELECT parser produces optional WHERE clause as Expression node
SELECT parser produces optional ORDER BY clause with column names and ASC/DESC
SELECT parser produces optional LIMIT clause with numeric value
INSERT parser produces AST with target table name
INSERT parser produces optional column name list before VALUES
INSERT parser produces VALUES clause with one or more tuples of expressions
CREATE TABLE parser extracts table name from statement
CREATE TABLE parser extracts column definitions with names and data types (INTEGER, TEXT, REAL, BLOB)
CREATE TABLE parser extracts column constraints (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
Expression parser correctly handles operator precedence: NOT > comparison > AND > OR
Binary expressions (AND, OR, comparison operators) produce BinaryExpression AST nodes
Unary NOT expression produces UnaryExpression AST node
Parser provides error position (line and column) for syntax errors
Parser reports meaningful error messages for unexpected tokens
NULL keyword is parsed as LiteralExpression not IdentifierExpression
String and numeric literals are parsed as LiteralExpression nodes
Column references are parsed as IdentifierExpression nodes
Test suite passes for 15+ valid SQL statements across SELECT, INSERT, and CREATE TABLE
Test suite correctly rejects 10+ invalid SQL statements with position information
pitfalls: Left recursion in expression grammar causes infinite recursion in recursive-descent parsers—use precedence climbing or Pratt parsing
AND binds tighter than OR in SQL (unlike some programming languages)—get this wrong and WHERE clauses evaluate incorrectly
Not handling parenthesized sub-expressions breaks complex WHERE clauses
NULL is a keyword, not an identifier—treat it specially in expression parsing
concepts: Recursive descent parsing uses mutually recursive functions for grammar rules
AST represents the syntactic structure of a SQL statement as a tree
Operator precedence determines evaluation order of expressions
Pratt parsing or precedence climbing handles binary operators elegantly
deliverables: SELECT statement parser producing AST
INSERT statement parser producing AST
CREATE TABLE parser producing AST with column definitions and constraints
Expression parser with correct operator precedence
Error reporting with token position for parse errors
Test suite for valid and invalid SQL inputs

milestones.name: Bytecode Compiler (VDBE)
milestones.description: Compile parsed AST into bytecode instructions executed by a virtual machine. This is the execution engine of the database.
acceptance_criteria: Compiler translates SELECT AST into a bytecode program with opcodes for OpenTable, Rewind, Column, ResultRow, Next, Halt
Compiler translates INSERT AST into bytecode with opcodes for OpenTable, MakeRecord, Insert, Halt
Virtual machine executes bytecode programs step-by-step, processing one opcode per cycle
VM maintains a register file (array of typed values) for intermediate computation
EXPLAIN command outputs the bytecode program for a given SQL statement in human-readable format
WHERE clause compiles to conditional jump opcodes that skip non-matching rows
Bytecode execution of 'SELECT * FROM t' on a 10,000-row table completes in under 100ms
Compiler translates SELECT AST into opcodes including OpenTable, Rewind, Column, ResultRow, Next, and Halt
Compiler translates INSERT AST into opcodes including OpenTable, MakeRecord, and Insert
VM executes bytecode in a fetch-decode-execute loop, processing one opcode per cycle
VM manages a register file of typed values for intermediate calculations
WHERE clauses are correctly compiled into conditional jump opcodes (e.g., Gt, Le, Ne)
The EXPLAIN command displays the human-readable opcode sequence for any valid SQL statement
The VM executes a full table scan of 10,000 rows in under 100ms
Bytecode execution of SELECT * FROM t on a 10,000-row table completes in under 100ms
pitfalls: Directly interpreting AST nodes (tree-walking interpreter) is simpler but significantly slower than bytecode—commit to bytecode
Register allocation must handle nested expressions without clobbering intermediate values
Opcodes for cursor management (open, rewind, next, close) must match the storage engine's iterator interface
Missing a Halt opcode causes the VM to run past the end of the program into garbage memory
concepts: Bytecode compilation translates high-level AST into low-level instruction sequence
Virtual machine executes bytecode using a fetch-decode-execute loop
Register-based VM uses a register file for operand storage (vs stack-based)
Cursor opcodes abstract B-tree traversal for the VM
deliverables: Bytecode instruction set with opcodes for table operations, comparisons, jumps, and output
Compiler translating SELECT, INSERT, CREATE TABLE ASTs into bytecode
Virtual machine executing bytecode with register file and program counter
EXPLAIN command displaying bytecode for any SQL statement
WHERE clause compilation to conditional jumps

milestones.name: Buffer Pool Manager
milestones.description: Implement a page cache that sits between the B-tree layer and disk, managing fixed-size pages with LRU eviction and dirty page tracking.
acceptance_criteria: Buffer pool manages a configurable number of in-memory page frames (default 1000 pages)
Pages are fixed-size (4096 bytes by default, configurable)
FetchPage loads a page from disk into a free frame, or returns the cached frame if already resident
LRU eviction selects the least recently used unpinned page for replacement when no free frames exist
Dirty page tracking marks pages modified in memory; eviction writes dirty pages to disk before replacement
Pin/Unpin mechanism prevents eviction of pages currently in use by B-tree operations
FlushAll writes all dirty pages to disk (used before checkpoint or shutdown)
Buffer pool hit rate is measurable and logged for performance tuning
Buffer pool initializes with a fixed number of 4096-byte frames
FetchPage returns the correct page from memory if already loaded (hit)
FetchPage loads page from disk if not in memory (miss)
LRU algorithm correctly identifies the least recently used page for eviction
Pinned pages (count > 0) are never selected for eviction
Dirty pages are written back to disk only when evicted or on FlushAll
Buffer pool hit rate is tracked and accessible for performance metrics
pitfalls: Evicting a pinned page causes data corruption or use-after-free—enforce pin counting
Not flushing dirty pages before eviction loses committed data
Page ID collisions if page numbering is not globally unique across the database file
Buffer pool deadlocks when B-tree operations pin too many pages simultaneously—set pin limits
concepts: Buffer pool caches disk pages in memory for fast access
LRU (Least Recently Used) eviction approximates optimal page replacement
Pin counting prevents eviction of actively-used pages
Dirty page tracking enables write-back caching
deliverables: Buffer pool with configurable frame count and page size
FetchPage loading pages from disk or returning cached frames
LRU eviction selecting least recently used unpinned page
Dirty page tracking and write-back on eviction
Pin/Unpin API for B-tree layer
FlushAll for shutdown and checkpoint

milestones.name: B-tree Page Format & Table Storage
milestones.description: Implement the on-disk page structure for B-trees (tables) and B+trees (indexes), with row serialization and node splitting.
acceptance_criteria: Page header contains page type (leaf/internal, table/index), cell count, free space pointer, and right-child pointer (internal only)
Table B-tree leaf pages store rows keyed by rowid with variable-length record encoding
Table B-tree internal pages store rowid separator keys and child page numbers
Index B+tree leaf pages store (indexed column value, rowid) pairs with data only in leaves
Index B+tree internal pages store only separator keys and child pointers (no row data)
CREATE TABLE creates a B-tree root page and records the schema in a system catalog (sqlite_master equivalent)
INSERT serializes a row and inserts into the correct B-tree leaf; node splitting creates a new page and promotes a separator key
Full table scan traverses all leaf pages in rowid order, returning all rows
Pages serialize to and deserialize from exactly 4096-byte buffers via the buffer pool
Page header correctly identifies Leaf vs Internal and Table vs Index types
Slotted page format implements bidirectional growth (pointers vs cells)
Table B-tree stores full records in leaf nodes keyed by rowid
Index B+tree stores key/rowid pairs only in leaf nodes
Node split algorithm correctly rebalances the tree and promotes keys to parents
Varint implementation handles 1-9 byte encoding for 64-bit integers
System catalog (sqlite_master) persists table root page numbers
Full table scan successfully iterates through all leaf pages in order
pitfalls: Conflating B-tree (data in all nodes) with B+tree (data only in leaves)—tables use B-tree, indexes use B+tree in SQLite
Cell overflow when a row exceeds page capacity requires overflow pages—handle or document the size limit
Page fragmentation after deletions wastes space—track free space within pages
Endianness must be consistent between write and read (SQLite uses big-endian for portability)
Variable-length integer encoding (varint) must handle the full range of 64-bit integers
concepts: B-tree stores key-value pairs in all nodes (used for rowid-keyed tables)
B+tree stores data only in leaf nodes with linked leaf pages (used for indexes)
Slotted page format uses cell pointers for variable-length records
Node splitting maintains B-tree balance on insert overflow
System catalog stores table and index schema metadata
deliverables: Page format with header, cell pointer array, and cell content area
Table B-tree with leaf (row storage) and internal (separator + child pointer) pages
Index B+tree with leaf (key + rowid) and internal (separator + child pointer) pages
Row serialization with variable-length encoding for column values
Node splitting on insert overflow with separator key promotion
System catalog table storing schema metadata
Full table scan via leaf page traversal

milestones.name: SELECT Execution & DML
milestones.description: Execute SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE via the bytecode VM, with row deserialization, projection, and filtering.
acceptance_criteria: SELECT * FROM table returns all rows in rowid order via B-tree leaf scan
SELECT col1, col2 returns only specified columns (projection)
WHERE clause filters rows during scan, evaluating boolean expressions on deserialized column values
INSERT adds a row to the B-tree; subsequent SELECT returns the inserted data
UPDATE modifies columns in rows matching WHERE; subsequent SELECT reflects changes
DELETE removes rows matching WHERE; subsequent SELECT no longer returns them
NOT NULL constraint rejects INSERT or UPDATE setting a NOT NULL column to null
Operations on non-existent tables return an error with the table name
SELECT * returns all rows by iterating through the B-tree leaf sequence
SELECT with column names correctly projects only the requested fields
WHERE clause correctly filters rows using Three-Valued Logic (handling NULLs)
INSERT adds a new row and updates the B-tree structure correctly
UPDATE and DELETE modify/remove rows while maintaining B-tree integrity
NOT NULL constraints reject invalid writes with a descriptive error
Attempting to query a table not in the System Catalog returns an 'undefined table' error
WHERE clause correctly filters rows using Three-Valued Logic (NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL, not TRUE)
Column projection correctly deserializes variable-length records to extract requested fields
INSERT with NULL for INTEGER PRIMARY KEY triggers auto-increment behavior
UPDATE cannot change the rowid (primary key) - must reject or handle as delete+insert
DELETE during iteration uses two-pass approach to avoid cursor corruption
pitfalls: Column name case sensitivity: SQL standard is case-insensitive for identifiers; be consistent
NULL handling in WHERE: NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (falsy), not TRUE—use IS NULL for null checks
B-tree rebalancing after DELETE is complex; initially, mark rows as deleted and reclaim space during compaction
Updating the primary key (rowid) requires delete + re-insert at the new position
Memory management for large result sets—stream results row-by-row, don't buffer all in memory
concepts: Cursor pattern abstracts B-tree traversal for the VM
Projection selects a subset of columns from each row
Predicate evaluation filters rows during scan
Three-valued logic (TRUE, FALSE, NULL) for SQL boolean expressions
deliverables: Table scan operator iterating all rows via B-tree cursor
Row deserialization from binary page format to typed column values
Column projection selecting specified fields
WHERE clause evaluation with three-valued logic
INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE execution via bytecode VM
NOT NULL and UNIQUE constraint enforcement during writes

milestones.name: Secondary Indexes
milestones.description: Implement secondary indexes using B+trees and integrate index lookups into query execution.
acceptance_criteria: CREATE INDEX builds a B+tree index mapping (indexed column value → rowid) from existing table data
Index is automatically maintained on INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE (index entries added/removed/updated)
Index lookup retrieves rows matching an equality predicate without full table scan, verified by counting pages read
Range scan on index returns rows within a value range using B+tree leaf traversal
Query execution uses index scan when an indexed column appears in WHERE with equality or range predicate
UNIQUE index rejects INSERT or UPDATE that would create duplicate values
CREATE INDEX builds a B+tree mapping column values to rowids
INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations maintain all associated indexes synchronously
Index lookup (equality) avoids full table scan and visits significantly fewer pages
Index range scan (BETWEEN or < >) traverses linked leaf pages
UNIQUE index correctly rejects duplicate value insertions
Bytecode VM can perform a 'Double Lookup' from index cursor to table cursor
pitfalls: Forgetting to update indexes on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE causes stale or missing index entries
Index on a column with many NULL values—NULLs must be handled consistently (SQLite allows multiple NULLs in UNIQUE index)
Composite indexes (multi-column) require careful key comparison—leftmost prefix must match for index to be useful
Index maintenance overhead can make writes slower; only create indexes that benefit read patterns
concepts: Secondary index maps indexed column values to primary keys (rowids)
B+tree leaf traversal enables efficient range scans
Index maintenance ensures indexes stay consistent with table data
Covering index can satisfy a query without table lookup if all needed columns are in the index
deliverables: CREATE INDEX building B+tree from existing table data
Index maintenance on INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
Index equality lookup avoiding full table scan
Index range scan using B+tree leaf traversal
UNIQUE index constraint enforcement

milestones.name: Query Planner & Statistics
milestones.description: Implement a cost-based query planner that chooses between table scan and index scan based on collected statistics.
acceptance_criteria: ANALYZE command collects statistics: row count per table, distinct value count per indexed column
Cost model estimates pages read for full table scan (total_pages) and index scan (estimated_rows / rows_per_page)
Planner selects index scan when estimated selectivity (matching_rows / total_rows) is below a threshold (e.g., 20%)
Planner falls back to table scan when no suitable index exists or selectivity is too low
EXPLAIN shows the chosen plan including scan type, index name (if used), and estimated row count
For multi-table queries (JOIN), planner estimates join cardinality and selects join order to minimize intermediate result size
ANALYZE command collects row count per table and distinct value count per indexed column
Cost model estimates pages read for full table scan based on total pages
Cost model estimates I/O cost for index scan based on selectivity and random I/O factor
Planner selects index scan when estimated selectivity is below threshold (e.g., 20%)
Planner falls back to table scan when no suitable index exists or selectivity is too high
EXPLAIN displays the chosen plan including scan type, index name if used, and estimated row count
For multi-table queries, planner estimates join cardinality and considers join order to minimize intermediate result size
pitfalls: Without statistics (before ANALYZE), the planner has no data for cost estimation—use default assumptions (e.g., assume 1M rows)
Stale statistics after many inserts/deletes cause the planner to choose suboptimal plans—recommend periodic ANALYZE
Cardinality estimation errors compound through joins—a 10x error in one table becomes 100x after a two-table join
Plan search space explodes exponentially with number of tables in JOIN—limit to dynamic programming for ≤10 tables
concepts: Cost-based optimization compares estimated cost of alternative plans
Cardinality estimation predicts the number of rows each operator produces
Selectivity is the fraction of rows matching a predicate
Statistics collection (ANALYZE) provides data for cost estimation
Dynamic programming-based join ordering for multi-table queries
deliverables: ANALYZE command collecting table and index statistics
Cost model estimating I/O for table scan vs index scan
Plan selection choosing cheapest access path per table
EXPLAIN command displaying chosen plan with cost estimates
Join order optimization for multi-table queries

milestones.name: Transactions (Rollback Journal)
milestones.description: Implement ACID transactions using a rollback journal for crash recovery.
acceptance_criteria: BEGIN starts a transaction; all subsequent writes are buffered until COMMIT or ROLLBACK
COMMIT makes all changes permanent by flushing dirty pages and removing the rollback journal
ROLLBACK undoes all changes by restoring original pages from the rollback journal
Rollback journal records original page contents BEFORE modification (for undo on crash)
Changes are not visible to other connections until COMMIT (basic read isolation)
Crash recovery on startup detects an existing rollback journal and automatically rolls back the incomplete transaction
Journal file is fsync'd before modified pages are written to the database file (write ordering guarantee)
BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK commands correctly toggle the engine state
A .db-journal file is created and contains original page data before any write to the main .db file
The journal file is physically flushed to disk (fsync) before the main database is modified
A manual ROLLBACK restores the state from the journal and clears the journal file
Startup logic detects a 'Hot Journal' and automatically restores the database to a consistent state
Writes are not visible to other database connections until the COMMIT is complete
BEGIN starts a transaction and buffers all subsequent writes until COMMIT or ROLLBACK
Rollback journal records original page contents BEFORE any modification to the database file
Changes are not visible to other database connections until COMMIT completes
BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK commands correctly toggle the transaction manager state
A manual ROLLBACK restores the database state from the journal and deletes the journal file
pitfalls: Writing modified pages to database before journal is fsync'd causes unrecoverable corruption on crash
Partial page writes (torn pages) on crash can corrupt the database—journal must contain complete original pages
Lock ordering between multiple connections must be consistent to prevent deadlocks
Long-running transactions holding locks block all other writers—document the locking behavior
concepts: Rollback journal records undo information (original page images) before modification

concepts.Write ordering: journal fsync → database write → journal delete

concepts.Crash recovery: hot journal detected → restore original pages → delete journal

deliverables: BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK command implementation
Rollback journal recording original page contents before modification
Write ordering ensuring journal is durable before database pages are modified
Crash recovery detecting hot journal and restoring database to pre-transaction state
ACID guarantee verification test suite

milestones.name: WAL Mode
milestones.description: Implement Write-Ahead Logging as an alternative to rollback journal, enabling concurrent readers during writes.
acceptance_criteria: WAL mode appends modified pages to a separate WAL file instead of modifying the main database file
Writers append to WAL; readers check WAL for the most recent version of a page before reading from the main database
Multiple readers can execute queries concurrently while a single writer appends to the WAL
Checkpoint (PRAGMA wal_checkpoint) copies WAL pages back into the main database file
WAL checkpoint is required to prevent unbounded WAL growth—auto-checkpoint triggers after configurable page count (default 1000)
Readers see a consistent snapshot: a reader that starts before a commit does not see that commit's changes (snapshot isolation for reads)
WAL file corruption is detected via page checksums
Writers append to a separate WAL file instead of modifying the main .db file
Readers search the WAL for the most recent page version before falling back to the main file
Writers and multiple readers can operate simultaneously without blocking
Checkpointing copies WAL pages to the main database and truncates the WAL
Automatic checkpoint triggers after 1000 pages (configurable)
Readers use a consistent snapshot based on the WAL state at their start time
Checksums are used to detect and reject corrupted WAL frames
pitfalls: WAL grows unbounded without checkpointing—auto-checkpoint is not optional, it's required
Readers pinning old WAL frames prevent checkpoint from truncating—long-running reads block WAL cleanup
WAL and rollback journal are mutually exclusive modes—switching requires careful state management
Checkpoint must not run while readers are using WAL frames that would be overwritten
WAL file corruption must be detected (checksums) to prevent propagating bad data to the main database
concepts: WAL appends redo information (new page images) to a log file
Readers use WAL index (wal-index) to find most recent page version
Checkpoint merges WAL changes back into the main database file

concepts.Snapshot isolation: each reader sees the database as of its start time

concepts.WAL vs rollback journal: WAL enables concurrent readers, rollback journal does not

deliverables: WAL file format appending modified pages with checksums
WAL reader looking up most recent page version before main database
Checkpoint process copying WAL pages into main database
Auto-checkpoint triggered by WAL page count threshold
Concurrent reader support during active write transactions
WAL mode toggle (PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL)

milestones.name: Aggregate Functions & JOIN
milestones.description: Implement aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX), GROUP BY, and basic JOIN execution.
acceptance_criteria: COUNT(*) returns the number of rows; COUNT(col) returns count of non-NULL values
SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX produce correct results over grouped and ungrouped queries
GROUP BY groups rows by specified columns before applying aggregate functions
HAVING filters groups after aggregation
INNER JOIN combines rows from two tables matching a join condition
Nested loop join is implemented as the baseline join algorithm
JOIN with WHERE clause filters correctly after join
COUNT(*) accurately counts rows including NULLs
COUNT(col) ignores NULL values in the target column
AVG returns a REAL/float even if input column is INTEGER
GROUP BY correctly partitions aggregate states into buckets
HAVING filters out aggregated groups based on result values
INNER JOIN correctly combines rows from two tables using a nested loop
JOIN with WHERE correctly filters rows before or during the join process
COUNT(*) returns the number of rows including NULLs
COUNT(col) returns count of non-NULL values only
SUM produces correct results over grouped and ungrouped queries
AVG returns REAL/float even if input column is INTEGER
AVG ignores NULL values in computation
MIN and MAX produce correct results over grouped and ungrouped queries
GROUP BY partitions rows into groups before applying aggregate functions
GROUP BY without ORDER BY can return groups in any order
HAVING filters groups after aggregation based on aggregate values
JOIN with WHERE clause filters correctly after or during join
Empty table with aggregates returns appropriate default values (0 for COUNT, NULL for SUM/AVG)
Multiple aggregates can be computed in a single query
pitfalls: AVG must handle integer division correctly (return REAL, not INTEGER)
NULL handling in aggregates: COUNT(*) counts NULLs, COUNT(col) does not; SUM/AVG ignore NULLs
GROUP BY without aggregate function is valid SQL but confusing—handle correctly
Nested loop join is O(n*m)—acceptable for small tables but document the limitation
concepts: Aggregate functions accumulate values across row groups
GROUP BY partitions rows into groups for aggregation
Nested loop join iterates all combinations of rows from two tables
HAVING filters groups after aggregation (vs WHERE which filters before)
deliverables: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX aggregate functions
GROUP BY execution with hash-based or sort-based grouping
HAVING clause filtering groups after aggregation
Nested loop INNER JOIN execution
Test suite covering aggregates with NULLs, empty tables, and multi-table joins

blueprint.title: Build Your Own SQLite
blueprint.overview: Build a complete embedded SQL database from scratch: tokenizer, recursive-descent parser, bytecode compiler (VDBE), buffer pool with LRU eviction, B-tree/B+tree page storage, query execution engine, secondary indexes, cost-based query planner, ACID transactions with rollback journal and WAL, and aggregate functions with JOIN execution. This expert-level project reveals how modern databases achieve durability, concurrency, and performance through layered architecture.
blueprint.design_philosophy: This project teaches the complete database stack by building each layer incrementally. Starting with text transformation (lexer→parser→bytecode), it builds upward to storage engines and downward to disk I/O. The key insight is that every database is a compiler: SQL is compiled to bytecode, executed by a VM, with data cached by a buffer pool and persisted via B-trees. Understanding this pipeline—token→AST→bytecode→execution→storage—is understanding all databases.

implementation.rationale: Direct memory control, manual page management, pointer arithmetic for binary formats, and matching SQLite's own implementation. C exposes the low-level details necessary for buffer pool management, page formats, and file I/O that higher-level languages would abstract away.
implementation.style_guide: snake_case for functions/variables, UPPER_CASE for macros, struct names Capitalized. Pages are fixed-size byte arrays. Function groups: xxx_new(), xxx_free(), xxx_read(), xxx_write(). Return 0 for success, negative error code for failure.
implementation.build_system: Makefile with targets: all, test, clean. Separate object files per module. Test runner executes SQL files and compares output.
alternatives: Rust: Zero-cost abstractions and memory safety, but less direct hardware control
Go: Good concurrency primitives, but GC adds latency unpredictability for storage engine

assumed_known: B-tree and B+tree data structures (search, insert, delete, balance)
SQL basics (SELECT, INSERT, CREATE TABLE, WHERE, JOIN)
File I/O (open, read, write, seek, fsync)
Basic compiler concepts (lexer, parser, AST, bytecode)

must_teach_first.concept: Slotted page format (cell pointers + cell content)

milestones.title: SQL Tokenizer
milestones.summary: Build a lexer that converts SQL text into typed tokens (keywords, identifiers, literals, operators, punctuation) using finite state machines.
milestones.misconception: Tokenization is trivial—just split on whitespace and recognize keywords. Many developers assume a simple regex or split() call suffices for SQL.
milestones.reveal: SQL tokenization requires a finite state machine that handles escaped quotes ('it''s'), case-insensitive keywords vs case-sensitive identifiers, double-quoted identifiers, multi-character operators (<=, >=, !=), and provides accurate line/column positions for error reporting.
cascade: State machines formalize lexical analysis — apply to any language lexer including config files, network protocols, and templating engines
Token classification enables syntax highlighting and IDE support — the same token types power language servers
Error position reporting requires tracking line/column state — essential for debugging tools and REPLs
Case folding for keywords is a locale-aware operation — connects to Unicode handling and internationalization
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Tokenizer recognizes SQL keywords case-insensitively
String literals handle escaped quotes ('' → ')
Numeric literals distinguish integers from floats
Operators (=, <, >, <=, >=, !=, <>) and punctuation are distinct tokens
Double-quoted identifiers are parsed as identifiers
Error position (line, column) reported for unrecognized characters
Test suite covers 20+ diverse SQL statements

milestones.title: SQL Parser (AST)
milestones.summary: Build a recursive-descent parser converting token stream into Abstract Syntax Trees for SELECT, INSERT, CREATE TABLE with correct operator precedence.
milestones.misconception: Parsing just checks if input is syntactically valid. The AST output is secondary—many think tree-walking interpretation is sufficient.
milestones.reveal: AST structure directly enables bytecode compilation. The tree shape determines instruction order. AND binds tighter than OR (reverse of C's && and ||). Left-recursion in grammar causes infinite recursion in recursive-descent parsers—precedence climbing solves this.
cascade: Recursive descent mirrors expression evaluation — understanding parser structure helps debug evaluation bugs
Operator precedence is everywhere — spreadsheet formulas, configuration languages, and programming languages all need precedence rules
AST is a universal intermediate representation — compilers for Python, JavaScript, and Rust all use similar tree structures
Grammar design affects error messages — good grammar enables 'expected X, got Y' style diagnostics
yaml_acceptance_criteria: SELECT parser produces AST with column list, FROM, optional WHERE/LIMIT
INSERT parser produces AST with table, column names, VALUES
CREATE TABLE parser extracts column definitions with types and constraints
Expression parser handles NOT > AND > OR precedence
Parenthesized expressions override precedence
Error messages include token position
Test suite: 15+ valid, 10+ invalid SQL statements

milestones.title: Bytecode Compiler (VDBE)
milestones.summary: Compile AST into bytecode instructions (opcodes) executed by a register-based virtual machine. Implements EXPLAIN for debugging.
milestones.misconception: Tree-walking interpretation (evaluate AST directly) is simpler and fast enough. Bytecode adds unnecessary complexity.
milestones.reveal: Bytecode decouples parsing from execution. One parse → many executions. Register allocation prevents value clobbering. Cursor opcodes abstract storage engine details. The fetch-decode-execute loop is the heart of every interpreter and CPU.
cascade: Bytecode VMs power modern computing — Python, Java, .NET, and Ethereum all use bytecode
Register allocation is a classic optimization problem — spills, conflicts, and live ranges matter
Instruction set design is architecture — opcode layout affects decode speed and code density
EXPLAIN enables query debugging — understanding execution plans is essential for performance tuning
yaml_acceptance_criteria: SELECT compiles to OpenTable, Rewind, Column, ResultRow, Next, Halt opcodes
INSERT compiles to OpenTable, MakeRecord, Insert opcodes
VM executes bytecode with register file and program counter
WHERE clause compiles to conditional jumps (Gt, Le, Ne)
EXPLAIN outputs human-readable opcode sequence
SELECT * FROM t with 10K rows completes in <100ms

milestones.title: Buffer Pool Manager
milestones.summary: Implement a page cache between B-tree layer and disk with LRU eviction, pin counting, and dirty page tracking.
milestones.misconception: Just rely on OS page cache (mmap) for everything. The OS already handles caching—database-level caching is redundant.
milestones.reveal: Database pages have semantic meaning (B-tree nodes, table data) that OS lacks. Database can prefetch related pages, pin pages during transactions, track dirty pages for write-back, and measure cache hit rate. OS page cache cannot do write-ahead logging coordination.
cascade: LRU is a classic cache algorithm — CPU caches, CDN, DNS resolvers all use variants
Pin counting is reference counting — similar to smart pointers and resource management
Dirty page tracking enables write-back caching — used in file systems, OS kernels, and game engines
Cache hit rate is a universal performance metric — applies to web servers, APIs, and databases
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Buffer pool manages configurable frames (default 1000)
Pages are fixed-size (default 4096 bytes)
FetchPage loads from disk or returns cached frame
LRU eviction selects least-recently-used unpinned page
Dirty pages written to disk on eviction or FlushAll
Pin/Unpin prevents eviction of pages in use
Buffer pool hit rate is logged

milestones.title: B-tree Page Format & Table Storage
milestones.summary: Implement on-disk page structure for B-trees (tables) and B+trees (indexes) with variable-length record encoding and node splitting.
milestones.misconception: B-tree and B+tree are interchangeable. All nodes store data. Pages are simple fixed-record arrays.
milestones.reveal: SQLite uses B-tree for tables (data in all nodes, keyed by rowid) but B+tree for indexes (data only in leaves, leaves linked). Pages use slotted format (cell pointers at end, content grows from start). Varints encode variable-length integers in 1-9 bytes. Node splitting promotes separator keys to maintain balance.
cascade: Page formats are binary serialization — similar to protocol buffers, FlatBuffers, Cap'n Proto
Varint encoding is in Google Protobuf, Redis, and many wire protocols — space-efficient integer storage
Slotted pages are used in SQL Server and PostgreSQL — understanding enables forensic database analysis
B-tree vs B+tree tradeoff is a storage systems fundamental — when to denormalize, when to index
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Page header: type, cell count, free space pointer, right-child (internal)
Table B-tree leaf: rows keyed by rowid, variable-length records
Table B-tree internal: separator keys + child page numbers
Index B+tree leaf: (indexed value, rowid) pairs, data only in leaves
Index B+tree internal: separator keys + child pointers, no data
Node splitting creates pages and promotes separator keys
Varint handles 1-9 byte encoding for 64-bit integers
Full table scan traverses all leaf pages in rowid order

milestones.title: SELECT Execution & DML
milestones.summary: Execute queries via bytecode VM with row deserialization, projection, filtering, and DML operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE).
milestones.misconception: Query execution is just 'iterate rows and filter.' NULL handling is straightforward (NULL = NULL is TRUE).
milestones.reveal: Cursor pattern abstracts B-tree traversal. Three-valued logic (TRUE, FALSE, NULL) means NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (falsy), not TRUE—use IS NULL for null checks. Projection selects columns from deserialized records. UPDATE changes columns; changing rowid requires delete+reinsert.
cascade: Cursors are everywhere — file iterators, network streams, and async iterators all use cursor patterns
Three-valued logic is SQL's most confusing feature — understanding it prevents subtle bugs
Projection vs filtering separates concerns — applies to API design, data pipelines, and ETL
Deserialization is a performance hotspot — zero-copy parsing (like Cap'n Proto) is an advanced optimization
yaml_acceptance_criteria: SELECT * returns all rows via B-tree leaf scan
SELECT col1, col2 projects specified columns only
WHERE filters rows during scan with three-valued logic
INSERT adds row to B-tree
UPDATE modifies columns, DELETE removes rows
NOT NULL constraint rejects invalid writes
Query non-existent table returns error

milestones.title: Secondary Indexes
milestones.summary: Implement B+tree secondary indexes with automatic maintenance on DML, equality/range lookups, and UNIQUE constraint enforcement.
milestones.misconception: Indexes are magic—they automatically speed up every query. Just add an index for every column.
milestones.reveal: Indexes have overhead (maintenance on writes). Equality lookups use index without full scan. Range scans traverse linked leaf pages. Composite indexes require leftmost prefix match. UNIQUE indexes reject duplicates. Index-only scans avoid table lookups if all columns are in the index.
cascade: Index selectivity determines usage — understanding when to index is a core database skill
Composite indexes mirror sorted columns — like multi-level sorting in spreadsheets
Covering indexes are a space-time tradeoff — common in read-heavy analytical workloads
Index maintenance is write amplification — the hidden cost of fast reads
yaml_acceptance_criteria: CREATE INDEX builds B+tree from existing table data
INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE maintain all indexes
Index equality lookup avoids full table scan
Index range scan traverses linked leaf pages
UNIQUE index rejects duplicate values
Double lookup: index cursor → table cursor

milestones.title: Query Planner & Statistics
milestones.summary: Implement cost-based query planner with ANALYZE statistics collection, cardinality estimation, and plan selection.
milestones.misconception: Query planners are optional—any plan works. Database always picks the best plan automatically.
milestones.reveal: Without statistics (before ANALYZE), planner assumes defaults (e.g., 1M rows). Cost = pages read × random I/O factor. Index scan selected when selectivity < threshold (~20%). Join order dramatically affects intermediate result size—dynamic programming finds optimal order for small joins.
cascade: Cost-based optimization is algorithmic — understanding cost models helps write faster queries
Cardinality estimation errors compound through joins — a 10x error becomes 100x in 2-way join
EXPLAIN is essential debugging — reading query plans is a required skill for backend developers
Dynamic programming is classic algorithmics — same technique used in sequence alignment and compilers
yaml_acceptance_criteria: ANALYZE collects row count per table, distinct values per indexed column
Cost model estimates I/O for table scan vs index scan
Planner selects index scan when selectivity below threshold
Planner falls back to table scan when no suitable index
EXPLAIN shows chosen plan, scan type, index name, estimated rows
Multi-table queries: planner estimates join cardinality and order

milestones.title: Transactions (Rollback Journal)
milestones.summary: Implement ACID transactions with rollback journal: BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK, write ordering, crash recovery.
milestones.misconception: Transactions just group operations. ACID is provided by the database. Just write data and it persists.
milestones.reveal: Rollback journal records original page images BEFORE modification (undo log). Write ordering: fsync journal → write database → delete journal. Crash recovery: detect hot journal → restore original pages → delete journal. Partial page writes (torn pages) are prevented by this ordering.
cascade: Write-ahead logging is universal — every database, every file system uses variants
fsync is the bottleneck — understanding durability vs performance is a systems fundamental
Crash recovery is forensic debugging — same principles apply to blockchain rollbacks
ACID is a contract — understanding guarantees prevents subtle application bugs
yaml_acceptance_criteria: BEGIN starts transaction, buffers writes until COMMIT/ROLLBACK
COMMIT makes changes permanent, flushes dirty pages, removes journal
ROLLBACK restores original pages from journal
Rollback journal records original page contents before modification
Changes invisible to other connections until COMMIT
Crash recovery detects hot journal and automatically rolls back
Write ordering: journal fsync → database write → journal delete

milestones.title: WAL Mode
milestones.summary: Implement Write-Ahead Logging as alternative to rollback journal, enabling concurrent readers during writes with checkpointing.
milestones.misconception: Single writer is sufficient—databases don't need concurrent write support. WAL is just a different journal format.
milestones.reveal: WAL appends new page images (redo log) to separate file. Readers check WAL for newest page version before main database (snapshot isolation). Multiple readers + single writer can proceed concurrently. Checkpoint copies WAL pages back to main database. Auto-checkpoint prevents unbounded WAL growth.
cascade: MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) is in PostgreSQL, MySQL, and distributed databases
Snapshot isolation is a key consistency level — understanding read skew vs write skew
WAL growth is a real operational concern — monitoring and maintenance are required
Checkpointing is a form of garbage collection — same principles apply to memory management
yaml_acceptance_criteria: WAL mode appends modified pages to separate WAL file
Writers append to WAL; readers check WAL for newest page first
Multiple readers execute while single writer appends to WAL
Checkpoint copies WAL pages to main database and truncates WAL
Auto-checkpoint triggers after 1000 pages
Readers see consistent snapshot at start time
WAL corruption detected via checksums

milestones.title: Aggregate Functions & JOIN
milestones.summary: Implement COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP BY, HAVING, and nested loop INNER JOIN.
milestones.misconception: Aggregates are simple counters—just iterate and add. JOIN is just cross-product + filter.
milestones.reveal: COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs; COUNT(col) ignores NULLs. AVG returns REAL even for INTEGER input (no integer division). GROUP BY partitions rows before aggregation—each group maintains separate accumulator state. HAVING filters groups after aggregation. Nested loop join is O(n×m)—acceptable for small tables.
cascade: Aggregation algorithms are fundamental — hash aggregation and sort aggregation are both used in production
NULL handling is SQL's trickiest feature — understanding three-valued logic prevents bugs
JOIN algorithms (nested loop, hash, merge) are core to query processing
Grouping is a partitioning problem — similar to MapReduce and parallel processing
yaml_acceptance_criteria: COUNT(*) returns row count including NULLs; COUNT(col) counts non-NULL
SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX correct for grouped and ungrouped queries
GROUP BY partitions rows before applying aggregates
HAVING filters groups after aggregation
INNER JOIN combines tables on join condition
Nested loop join implemented as baseline algorithm
Empty tables with aggregates return defaults (0 for COUNT, NULL for SUM)

diagrams.title: SQLite Architecture Overview (Satellite Map)
diagrams.description: Complete system architecture showing SQL text → Tokenizer → Parser → AST → Bytecode Compiler → VDBE → Storage Engine (Buffer Pool + B-trees) → Disk. Shows the two-tier VM architecture (SQL compiler + storage VM). This is the 'Home Base' diagram all other milestones reference.

diagrams.title: Tokenizer Finite State Machine
diagrams.description: State transition diagram for SQL tokenization: START → (keyword/identifier/string/number/operator/punctuation) → END. Shows handling of escaped quotes ('') and double-quoted identifiers.

diagrams.title: Token Type Taxonomy
diagrams.description: Tree diagram of token classification: Keywords (DML, DDL), Identifiers (quoted, unquoted), Literals (string, integer, float, blob), Operators, Punctuation. Shows example tokens for each type.

diagrams.title: AST Node Types
diagrams.description: Structure layout showing all AST node types: SelectStatement, InsertStatement, CreateTableStatement, Expression (Binary, Unary, Literal, Identifier). Shows parent-child relationships and field names.

diagrams.title: Operator Precedence Climbing
diagrams.description: Precedence levels for SQL: NOT (highest) → comparison (=,<,>) → AND → OR (lowest). Shows parse flow for 'a = 1 AND b = 2 OR c = 3' grouping as '(a=1 AND b=2) OR c=3'.

diagrams.title: Recursive Descent Parse Flow
diagrams.description: Call graph showing parse_statement → parse_select / parse_insert / parse_create_table. Expression parsing uses precedence climbing. Shows function call hierarchy.

diagrams.title: VDBE Architecture
diagrams.description: Block diagram of VM: Program Counter → Instruction Fetch → Decode → Execute → Register File → Cursor Interface → Loop. Shows how bytecode instructions manipulate the register file and interact with storage cursors.

diagrams.title: Opcode Categories
diagrams.description: Organized list of VDBE opcodes: Cursor ops (Open, Rewind, Next, Close), Record ops (Column, MakeRecord), Control flow (Goto, If, Halt), Comparison (Eq, Lt, Gt), Aggregation. Shows operand format.

diagrams.title: SELECT Bytecode Trace
diagrams.description: Step-by-step bytecode trace for 'SELECT * FROM t WHERE id = 1': OpenTable(0,t) → Rewind(0) → Column(0,0) → Gt(1,10) → Next(0,-4) → ResultRow(...) → Halt. Shows register values at each step.

diagrams.title: Buffer Pool State Machine
diagrams.description: State diagram of buffer pool frames: FREE → CLEAN (pinned/unpinned) → DIRTY. Transitions: FetchPage, Pin, Unpin, MarkDirty, Evict (flush if dirty). Shows LRU list ordering.

diagrams.title: LRU Eviction Algorithm
diagrams.description: Data structure layout: Hash table (page_id → frame), doubly-linked list (MRU ← → LRU). Eviction selects tail, unlinks, writes if dirty, hashes new page in at head. Shows pin count preventing eviction.

diagrams.title: Buffer Pool Hit Rate Calculation
diagrams.description: Formula: hit_rate = hits / (hits + misses). Shows instrumentation points in FetchPage. Example: 950 hits + 50 misses = 95% hit rate. Performance impact visualization.

diagrams.title: Page Header Format
diagrams.description: Byte layout of 4096-byte page: 2-byte page type, 2-byte cell count, 2-byte free space start, 2-byte fragmented bytes, 4-byte rightmost pointer (internal only), then cell pointer array (growing backward), then cell content (growing forward).

diagrams.title: Varint Encoding
diagrams.description: Varint bit layout: continuation bit (0x80) on bytes 2-9 indicates more bytes. First byte: 7 bits of data. Shows encoding examples: 300 = 0xAC 0x02 (10101100 00000010). Compare to 8-byte fixed encoding.

diagrams.title: B-tree vs B+tree Structure
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison: B-tree (data in all nodes, keyed by rowid) vs B+tree (data only in leaves, leaves linked). Shows table B-tree with row data in internal nodes vs index B+tree with only keys in internal nodes.

diagrams.title: Slotted Page Cell Format
diagrams.description: Cell layout for table leaf: payload length (varint), rowid (varint), payload (column values varint-encoded). Cell pointer array at page end points to cell starts. Shows free space calculation: page_size - header - pointer_array - total_cell_space.

diagrams.title: B-tree Node Split
diagrams.description: Before/after visualization: Full page with N cells splits into two pages at median key. New right page gets upper N/2 cells. Median separator key promoted to parent. Shows cascading split if parent is also full.

diagrams.title: System Catalog Structure
diagrams.description: sqlite_master equivalent table schema: columns (type, name, tbl_name, rootpage, sql). Shows how CREATE TABLE inserts row into catalog, how catalog lookup resolves table names during query compilation.

diagrams.title: B-tree Cursor Operations
diagrams.description: Cursor interface: Open(table_id) → position at first row → while(Next()) { Process(Row) } → Close. Shows how cursor maintains position across B-tree leaf traversal, how WHERE filtering integrates.

diagrams.title: Three-Valued Logic Truth Tables
diagrams.description: Truth tables for SQL operators with NULL: AND (T∧T=T, T∧F=F, T∧N=N), OR (T∨F=T, F∨F=F, N∨F=N), NOT (¬T=F, ¬F=T, ¬N=N). Shows why 'WHERE x = NULL' never matches—use IS NULL instead.

diagrams.title: Row Record Deserialization
diagrams.description: Reverse of cell encoding: Read varint payload length → read varint rowid → read each column value using type byte. Shows column projection: deserialize all, then extract only requested columns to avoid wasted work.

diagrams.title: INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE Execution Flow
diagrams.description: INSERT flow: Parse → Compile → VM executes MakeRecord → Cursor.Insert → Index updates → Txn log. UPDATE flow: Find row via cursor → Modify in place → Mark dirty. DELETE flow: Mark deleted (tombstone) or remove cells → Index cleanup.

diagrams.title: Secondary Index B+tree
diagrams.description: Index B+tree for 'CREATE INDEX idx ON t(name)': Leaf nodes store (name_value, rowid) pairs, sorted by name. Internal nodes store (name_separator, child_page). Leaves linked for range scans. Shows double lookup: index cursor → rowid → table cursor.

diagrams.title: Index Lookup Execution
diagrams.description: Equality lookup 'WHERE name = Alice': Traverse index B+tree to leaf → scan for matching (name, rowid) pairs → for each match, lookup row in table B+tree. Shows pages read: 1 (index root) + 3 (index path) + 1 (table leaf) = 5 pages vs 1000 for full scan.

diagrams.title: Index Range Scan
diagrams.description: Range lookup 'WHERE name BETWEEN Alice AND Bob': Find starting leaf via tree traversal → scan forward through linked leaves collecting (name, rowid) pairs → table lookups for each. Shows continuation until name > Bob.

diagrams.title: Composite Index Ordering
diagrams.description: Composite index on (a, b, c): rows sorted first by a, then by b within each a, then by c within each (a,b). Shows leftmost prefix rule: WHERE a=1 AND b>5 can use index, but WHERE b=5 cannot—b is not leftmost.

diagrams.title: Query Plan Selection Flow
diagrams.description: Decision tree: For each table → Get statistics → Estimate rows per access path (table scan vs index scan) → Compare costs → Select cheapest. For multi-table: Enumerate join orders → Estimate cardinalities → Pick best. Shows cost formula: pages × random_io_factor.

diagrams.title: Statistics Collection (ANALYZE)
diagrams.description: ANALYZE scans each table/index → counts rows, builds histogram of value distribution per column → stores in sqlite_stat table. Shows example: t(id) has 10000 rows, id values 1-10000 uniform, idx(name) has 50 distinct names.

diagrams.title: EXPLAIN Plan Output
diagrams.description: Example EXPLAIN output for 'SELECT * FROM t JOIN u ON t.id = u.tid WHERE t.x = 5': SCAN t USING INDEX idx_x → SCAN u → COMPOUND SUBQUERY. Shows columns: id, parent, not_used, opcode, p1, p2, p3, comment.

diagrams.title: Join Order Optimization
diagrams.description: Dynamic programming table for 3 tables (A, B, C): Best A = A (full scan), Best AB = A×B, Best ABC = (A×B)×C. Shows how optimal order minimizes intermediate results: joining small table first reduces memory.

diagrams.title: Transaction State Machine
diagrams.description: State diagram: IDLE → (BEGIN) → ACTIVE → (COMMIT → IDLE) or (ROLLBACK → IDLE). Shows how BEGIN starts accumulating changes, COMMIT makes durable, ROLLBACK discards. Shows auto-commit behavior when not in explicit transaction.

diagrams.title: Rollback Journal Format
diagrams.description: Journal file structure: 12-byte header (magic, page count, sector size, device flags) + N original page images. Each page: original content (4096 bytes). Shows write ordering: journal ← database, then database ← journal deleted.

diagrams.title: Write Ordering Guarantees
diagrams.description: Timeline: 1) Begin txn, 2) Read page, 3) Modify in buffer pool (dirty), 4) Write original to journal, 5) fsync(journal), 6) Write modified to database, 7) fsync(database), 8) Delete journal. Shows why step 5 before 6 prevents torn pages.

diagrams.title: Crash Recovery Procedure
diagrams.description: Startup flow: Open database → Check for .db-journal → If exists: Hot Journal! → Read journal header → For each page: write original to database → Delete journal → Open normally. Shows state restored to pre-transaction point.

diagrams.title: WAL File Format
diagrams.description: WAL frame structure: 24-byte frame header (page number, commit size, salt) + 4-byte checksum + 4096-byte page. WAL file is append-only sequence of frames. Shows how checkpoint frames differ from commit frames (commit size in header).

diagrams.title: WAL Reader/Writer Concurrency
diagrams.description: State diagram: Writer holds WAL lock exclusively → Appends frames → Commits by writing frame commit size. Readers each have transaction start frame number → Search WAL from end (newest) backward → Use first matching version. Shows concurrent reader + writer operation.

diagrams.title: WAL Snapshot Isolation
diagrams.description: Reader A starts at frame 100, reads pages. Writer commits frames 101-105. Reader A still sees frame 100 versions—doesn't see writer's changes. This is snapshot isolation. Shows how readers don't block writers and vice versa.

diagrams.title: Checkpoint Process
diagrams.description: Checkpoint flow: 1) Acquire exclusive lock on database, 2) Find last committed frame in WAL, 3) Copy frames 1 through last_committed to database pages, 4) Truncate WAL to empty, 5) Release lock. Shows auto-checkpoint triggers after N frames (default 1000).

diagrams.title: Aggregate State Machine
diagrams.description: Per-group accumulator state: COUNT: integer, SUM: float, AVG: (sum, count), MIN: value, MAX: value. Shows how GROUP BY creates new accumulator for each group. Initial state: COUNT=0, SUM=0, MIN=NULL, MAX=NULL.

diagrams.title: GROUP BY Execution
diagrams.description: Two approaches: Sort-based (sort rows by group keys, then scan computing aggregates) vs Hash-based (hash table keyed by group keys, accumulate into buckets). Shows trade-offs: sort-based uses less memory for large datasets, hash-based faster for medium datasets.

diagrams.title: Nested Loop Join Algorithm
diagrams.description: Pseudocode: For each row in outer table: For each row in inner table: If join condition matches: output combined row. Shows complexity O(n×m). Shows optimization: put smaller table outer, use index on inner for equality joins.

diagrams.title: NULL Handling in Aggregates
diagrams.description: Comparison table: COUNT(*) = 5 (includes NULLs), COUNT(col) = 3 (ignores NULLs), SUM(1,2,NULL,4) = 7 (ignores NULL), AVG(1,2,NULL,4) = 2.333 (ignores NULL, computes sum/count). Shows empty result: COUNT returns 0, others return NULL.

accumulated_md:
# Build Your Own SQLite

Build a complete embedded SQL database from scratch: tokenizer, recursive-descent parser, bytecode compiler (VDBE), buffer pool with LRU eviction, B-tree/B+tree page storage, query execution engine, secondary indexes, cost-based query planner, ACID transactions with rollback journal and WAL, and aggregate functions with JOIN execution. This expert-level project reveals how modern databases achieve durability, concurrency, and performance through layered architecture.

diagrams_to_generate.title: SQLite Architecture Overview (Satellite Map)
diagrams_to_generate.description: Complete system architecture showing SQL text → Tokenizer → Parser → AST → Bytecode Compiler → VDBE → Storage Engine (Buffer Pool + B-trees) → Disk. Shows the two-tier VM architecture (SQL compiler + storage VM). This is the 'Home Base' diagram all other milestones reference.

diagrams_to_generate.description: Step-by-step bytecode trace for 'SELECT * FROM t WHERE id = 1': OpenTable(0,t) → Rewind(0) → Column(0,0) → Gt(1,10) → Next(0,-4) → ResultRow(...) → Halt. Shows register values at each step.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Buffer Pool Hit Rate Calculation
diagrams_to_generate.description: Formula: hit_rate = hits / (hits + misses). Shows instrumentation points in FetchPage. Example: 950 hits + 50 misses = 95% hit rate. Performance impact visualization.

diagrams_to_generate.description: Byte layout of 4096-byte page: 2-byte page type, 2-byte cell count, 2-byte free space start, 2-byte fragmented bytes, 4-byte rightmost pointer (internal only), then cell pointer array (growing backward), then cell content (growing forward).

diagrams_to_generate.description: Side-by-side comparison: B-tree (data in all nodes, keyed by rowid) vs B+tree (data only in leaves, leaves linked). Shows table B-tree with row data in internal nodes vs index B+tree with only keys in internal nodes.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Slotted Page Cell Format
diagrams_to_generate.description: Cell layout for table leaf: payload length (varint), rowid (varint), payload (column values varint-encoded). Cell pointer array at page end points to cell starts. Shows free space calculation: page_size - header - pointer_array - total_cell_space.

diagrams_to_generate.title: B-tree Node Split
diagrams_to_generate.description: Before/after visualization: Full page with N cells splits into two pages at median key. New right page gets upper N/2 cells. Median separator key promoted to parent. Shows cascading split if parent is also full.

diagrams_to_generate.description: sqlite_master equivalent table schema: columns (type, name, tbl_name, rootpage, sql). Shows how CREATE TABLE inserts row into catalog, how catalog lookup resolves table names during query compilation.

diagrams_to_generate.description: Cursor interface: Open(table_id) → position at first row → while(Next()) { Process(Row) } → Close. Shows how cursor maintains position across B-tree leaf traversal, how WHERE filtering integrates.

diagrams_to_generate.description: Truth tables for SQL operators with NULL: AND (T∧T=T, T∧F=F, T∧N=N), OR (T∨F=T, F∨F=F, N∨F=N), NOT (¬T=F, ¬F=T, ¬N=N). Shows why 'WHERE x = NULL' never matches—use IS NULL instead.

diagrams_to_generate.description: Reverse of cell encoding: Read varint payload length → read varint rowid → read each column value using type byte. Shows column projection: deserialize all, then extract only requested columns to avoid wasted work.

diagrams_to_generate.title: INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE Execution Flow
diagrams_to_generate.description: INSERT flow: Parse → Compile → VM executes MakeRecord → Cursor.Insert → Index updates → Txn log. UPDATE flow: Find row via cursor → Modify in place → Mark dirty. DELETE flow: Mark deleted (tombstone) or remove cells → Index cleanup.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Secondary Index B+tree
diagrams_to_generate.description: Index B+tree for 'CREATE INDEX idx ON t(name)': Leaf nodes store (name_value, rowid) pairs, sorted by name. Internal nodes store (name_separator, child_page). Leaves linked for range scans. Shows double lookup: index cursor → rowid → table cursor.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Index Lookup Execution
diagrams_to_generate.description: Equality lookup 'WHERE name = Alice': Traverse index B+tree to leaf → scan for matching (name, rowid) pairs → for each match, lookup row in table B+tree. Shows pages read: 1 (index root) + 3 (index path) + 1 (table leaf) = 5 pages vs 1000 for full scan.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Index Range Scan
diagrams_to_generate.description: Range lookup 'WHERE name BETWEEN Alice AND Bob': Find starting leaf via tree traversal → scan forward through linked leaves collecting (name, rowid) pairs → table lookups for each. Shows continuation until name > Bob.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Composite Index Ordering
diagrams_to_generate.description: Composite index on (a, b, c): rows sorted first by a, then by b within each a, then by c within each (a,b). Shows leftmost prefix rule: WHERE a=1 AND b>5 can use index, but WHERE b=5 cannot—b is not leftmost.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Query Plan Selection Flow
diagrams_to_generate.description: Decision tree: For each table → Get statistics → Estimate rows per access path (table scan vs index scan) → Compare costs → Select cheapest. For multi-table: Enumerate join orders → Estimate cardinalities → Pick best. Shows cost formula: pages × random_io_factor.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Statistics Collection (ANALYZE)
diagrams_to_generate.description: ANALYZE scans each table/index → counts rows, builds histogram of value distribution per column → stores in sqlite_stat table. Shows example: t(id) has 10000 rows, id values 1-10000 uniform, idx(name) has 50 distinct names.

diagrams_to_generate.title: EXPLAIN Plan Output
diagrams_to_generate.description: Example EXPLAIN output for 'SELECT * FROM t JOIN u ON t.id = u.tid WHERE t.x = 5': SCAN t USING INDEX idx_x → SCAN u → COMPOUND SUBQUERY. Shows columns: id, parent, not_used, opcode, p1, p2, p3, comment.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Join Order Optimization
diagrams_to_generate.description: Dynamic programming table for 3 tables (A, B, C): Best A = A (full scan), Best AB = A×B, Best ABC = (A×B)×C. Shows how optimal order minimizes intermediate results: joining small table first reduces memory.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Transaction State Machine
diagrams_to_generate.description: State diagram: IDLE → (BEGIN) → ACTIVE → (COMMIT → IDLE) or (ROLLBACK → IDLE). Shows how BEGIN starts accumulating changes, COMMIT makes durable, ROLLBACK discards. Shows auto-commit behavior when not in explicit transaction.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Rollback Journal Format
diagrams_to_generate.description: Journal file structure: 12-byte header (magic, page count, sector size, device flags) + N original page images. Each page: original content (4096 bytes). Shows write ordering: journal ← database, then database ← journal deleted.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Write Ordering Guarantees
diagrams_to_generate.description: Timeline: 1) Begin txn, 2) Read page, 3) Modify in buffer pool (dirty), 4) Write original to journal, 5) fsync(journal), 6) Write modified to database, 7) fsync(database), 8) Delete journal. Shows why step 5 before 6 prevents torn pages.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Crash Recovery Procedure
diagrams_to_generate.description: Startup flow: Open database → Check for .db-journal → If exists: Hot Journal! → Read journal header → For each page: write original to database → Delete journal → Open normally. Shows state restored to pre-transaction point.

diagrams_to_generate.title: WAL File Format
diagrams_to_generate.description: WAL frame structure: 24-byte frame header (page number, commit size, salt) + 4-byte checksum + 4096-byte page. WAL file is append-only sequence of frames. Shows how checkpoint frames differ from commit frames (commit size in header).

diagrams_to_generate.title: WAL Reader/Writer Concurrency
diagrams_to_generate.description: State diagram: Writer holds WAL lock exclusively → Appends frames → Commits by writing frame commit size. Readers each have transaction start frame number → Search WAL from end (newest) backward → Use first matching version. Shows concurrent reader + writer operation.

diagrams_to_generate.title: WAL Snapshot Isolation
diagrams_to_generate.description: Reader A starts at frame 100, reads pages. Writer commits frames 101-105. Reader A still sees frame 100 versions—doesn't see writer's changes. This is snapshot isolation. Shows how readers don't block writers and vice versa.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Checkpoint Process
diagrams_to_generate.description: Checkpoint flow: 1) Acquire exclusive lock on database, 2) Find last committed frame in WAL, 3) Copy frames 1 through last_committed to database pages, 4) Truncate WAL to empty, 5) Release lock. Shows auto-checkpoint triggers after N frames (default 1000).

diagrams_to_generate.title: Aggregate State Machine
diagrams_to_generate.description: Per-group accumulator state: COUNT: integer, SUM: float, AVG: (sum, count), MIN: value, MAX: value. Shows how GROUP BY creates new accumulator for each group. Initial state: COUNT=0, SUM=0, MIN=NULL, MAX=NULL.

diagrams_to_generate.title: GROUP BY Execution
diagrams_to_generate.description: Two approaches: Sort-based (sort rows by group keys, then scan computing aggregates) vs Hash-based (hash table keyed by group keys, accumulate into buckets). Shows trade-offs: sort-based uses less memory for large datasets, hash-based faster for medium datasets.

diagrams_to_generate.title: Nested Loop Join Algorithm
diagrams_to_generate.description: Pseudocode: For each row in outer table: For each row in inner table: If join condition matches: output combined row. Shows complexity O(n×m). Shows optimization: put smaller table outer, use index on inner for equality joins.

diagrams_to_generate.title: NULL Handling in Aggregates
diagrams_to_generate.description: Comparison table: COUNT(*) = 5 (includes NULLs), COUNT(col) = 3 (ignores NULLs), SUM(1,2,NULL,4) = 7 (ignores NULL), AVG(1,2,NULL,4) = 2.333 (ignores NULL, computes sum/count). Shows empty result: COUNT returns 0, others return NULL.
